attrs.alt: Tenant Restrictions v2 — Traffic Inspection Architecture

content.text: What are Tenant Restrictions?

Tenant Restrictions (TR) is a control that lets an organization's IT department prevent employees from using corporate-managed networks or devices to sign in to unauthorized Azure AD tenants. The primary use case is preventing data exfiltration: an employee should not be able to copy company data to their personal OneDrive, a shadow-IT SaaS app, or a tenant belonging to an unknown third party.

Tenant Restrictions does not block access to the internet — it blocks authentication to non-allowed tenants via Entra ID sign-in. A user can still browse the web; they simply cannot sign in using a corporate-network token to tenants outside the approved list.
Tenant Restrictions v1 vs v2
Aspect
v1 (HTTP Header Injection)
v2 (Global Secure Access / SSE)
Enforcement point
Corporate HTTP proxy — injects headers into traffic to login.microsoftonline.com
Microsoft Entra Internet Access SSE agent or partner proxy
What it controls
Sign-in only — cannot block already-cached tokens
Both sign-in AND token usage; enforced at Microsoft's edge
Coverage
On-network only (proxy must be in path)
On-network and remote (agent on device)
Personal MSA blocking
Partial
Full — including outlook.com, Xbox accounts
Required license
None — proxy-based, free
Microsoft Entra Internet Access (part of Entra Suite)
Configuration location
Cross-Tenant Access Settings + proxy config
Microsoft Entra admin center — Global Secure Access
Header used
Restrict-Access-To-Tenants + Restrict-Access-Context
Sec-Restrict-Tenant-Access-Policy (v2 signal)
Configuring Tenant Restrictions v1
For v1, your corporate proxy intercepts HTTPS traffic to login.microsoftonline.com and injects two HTTP headers. TLS inspection (SSL break-and-inspect) is required since Entra ID endpoints use HTTPS.
Required HTTP Headers

# Headers injected by the corporate proxy on all requests to login.microsoftonline.com:

# Header 1: comma-separated list of allowed tenant IDs or domain names
Restrict-Access-To-Tenants: contoso.onmicrosoft.com,partner.com,<tenant-guid>

# Header 2: your organization's own tenant ID (identifies the restricting policy owner)
Restrict-Access-Context: <your-org-tenant-id>

# To also allow Microsoft personal accounts (MSA) — add:
# Restrict-Access-To-Tenants: ...,9188040d-6c67-4c5b-b112-36a304b66dad
# The GUID above is the well-known MSA tenant ID — omit it to block personal accounts
Squid Proxy Configuration Example

# /etc/squid/squid.conf — add these lines to inject TR headers

# ACL matching Microsoft login endpoints
acl mslogin dstdomain .login.microsoftonline.com .login.microsoft.com

# Inject the tenant restriction headers on matching traffic
request_header_add Restrict-Access-To-Tenants "contoso.onmicrosoft.com,partner.com" mslogin
request_header_add Restrict-Access-Context "aaaabbbb-0000-1111-2222-ccccddddeeee" mslogin

# Ensure TLS inspection is active for the login domain
ssl_bump stare mslogin
ssl_bump bump mslogin
Configuring Tenant Restrictions in Cross-Tenant Access Settings
Configuring Tenant Restrictions v2

Tenant Restrictions v2 is configured in the Microsoft Entra admin center under Global Secure Access. It leverages the Entra Internet Access agent installed on managed devices or a compatible SSE partner proxy.
Enabling TRv2 via PowerShell (Preview API)
Per-Tenant Exceptions

The default Tenant Restrictions policy blocks all non-listed tenants. You can carve out exceptions for specific external tenants — allowing access to a specific partner's M365 while still blocking all other external tenants.
Auditing Tenant Restriction Violations
KQL: Sign-in Blocks from Tenant Restrictions

// Sign-in failures caused by tenant restriction policy — last 7 days
SignInLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(7d)
| where ResultType == 900432  // AADSTS900432 — blocked by tenant restrictions
| project
    TimeGenerated,
    UserPrincipalName,
    AppDisplayName,
    ResourceTenantId,
    IPAddress,
    Location = tostring(LocationDetails.city)
| order by TimeGenerated desc
KQL: Top Blocked External Tenants

// Which external tenants are employees most frequently attempting to sign in to?
SignInLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(30d)
| where ResultType == 900432
| summarize AttemptCount = count(), UniqueUsers = dcount(UserId)
    by ResourceTenantId, AppDisplayName
| order by AttemptCount desc
| take 20
Common Issues
Symptom
Cause
Fix
AADSTS900432 for all external sign-ins
Header injected but allowed list missing your org's own tenant ID
Add your own tenant ID to the Restrict-Access-To-Tenants header
Restriction not enforced off-network
v1 proxy-based — only works when traffic routes through the proxy
Deploy Tenant Restrictions v2 with Entra Internet Access agent for remote workers
Microsoft Teams breaks for guest access
B2B guest sign-in blocked by default policy
Add the guest's home tenant to the per-partner exception list in CTAS
Personal OneDrive still accessible
MSA tenant GUID not in the block list
Omit 9188040d-6c67-4c5b-b112-36a304b66dad from allowed list to block MSA
TR blocks internal apps on shared devices
Shared-device sign-in routes through proxy without user context
Create a bypass ACL in the proxy for internal apps by IP or hostname
Configuring Tenant Restrictions v1 on a Squid Proxy

Tenant Restrictions v1 works by injecting two HTTP headers on every HTTPS request destined for login.microsoftonline.com. The proxy performs TLS inspection (SSL bump) to read and modify the request headers before they reach Microsoft's authentication servers.
Squid Proxy Configuration

# /etc/squid/squid.conf — Tenant Restrictions v1 configuration
# Requires ssl_bump (TLS interception) for login.microsoftonline.com

# Define the ACL for Microsoft authentication endpoints
acl ms_login dstdomain .login.microsoftonline.com
acl ms_login dstdomain .login.windows.net
acl ms_login dstdomain .login.microsoft.com

# SSL bump configuration — intercept TLS for the login endpoints only
ssl_bump peek step1 all
ssl_bump stare step2 ms_login
ssl_bump bump step3 ms_login
ssl_bump splice step3 all

# Inject Tenant Restrictions headers
# Replace <your-tenant-id> with your corporate Entra tenant ID
# Replace <policy-guid> with the GUID of the Cross-Tenant Access Policy
request_header_add Restrict-Access-To-Tenants '<your-tenant-id>,<allowed-tenant-id-2>' ms_login
request_header_add Restrict-Access-Context '<your-tenant-id>' ms_login

# Remove any client-supplied TR headers (prevents bypass attempts)
request_header_access Restrict-Access-To-Tenants deny all
request_header_access Restrict-Access-Context deny all

# Apply the inject after the remove (Squid processes in order)
request_header_add Restrict-Access-To-Tenants '<your-tenant-id>' ms_login
request_header_add Restrict-Access-Context '<your-tenant-id>' ms_login

# Standard Squid proxy listener
http_port 3128
https_port 3129 ssl-bump cert=/etc/squid/corporate-ca.pem key=/etc/squid/corporate-ca.key
Tenant Restrictions v2 — Cross-Tenant Access Policy (PowerShell)

Tenant Restrictions v2 (TRv2) is configured entirely within Entra ID as part of the Cross-Tenant Access Settings. The policy signals are embedded in authentication tokens rather than HTTP headers, enabling enforcement even for client apps that do not go through a corporate proxy. Enforcement requires either Global Secure Access (Entra Internet Access) or a compatible proxy that sets the sec-Restrict-Access-To-Tenants header.
Configure TRv2 Default Policy
Add a Per-Tenant Exception
Tenant Restrictions via Global Secure Access (Entra Internet Access)

Microsoft Entra Internet Access is the recommended enforcement mechanism for TRv2. It acts as a cloud-delivered Secure Web Gateway (SWG) that routes authentication traffic through Microsoft's network where TRv2 policy signals are applied natively — no on-premises proxy required.
Deploy the Global Secure Access client (Windows/macOS/Linux/iOS/Android) to employee devices via Intune or your MDM.
Enable the Microsoft 365 traffic profile in Entra Internet Access to route Microsoft authentication traffic through the GSA tunnel.
Tenant Restrictions v2 policy is evaluated at the GSA edge using the Cross-Tenant Access Policy configured in your home tenant.
Unlike proxy-based TRv1, GSA enforcement covers all apps including thick clients (Teams desktop, Outlook), not just browser sessions.
Conditional Access policies can block access from devices that are not running the GSA client (device compliance check), ensuring all enterprise devices route through GSA.
Audit logs appear in the Microsoft Entra admin center under Global Secure Access > Logs > Traffic logs.
GSA Client Deployment via Intune (PowerShell)

Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'DeviceManagementApps.ReadWrite.All'

# Query enrolled Windows devices that have GSA client installed
$gsaAppId = 'a0c73c16-a7e3-4564-9a95-2bdf47383716'  # Global Secure Access client app ID

$devices = Get-MgDeviceManagementManagedDevice -Filter "operatingSystem eq 'Windows'"
Write-Host "Total enrolled Windows devices: $($devices.Count)"

# Check compliance — devices without GSA should be flagged
# In Intune, create a compliance policy requiring GSA client
# Then target a Conditional Access policy: require compliant device

# View current tenant restrictions enforcement mode
$trPolicy = Get-MgPolicyCrossTenantAccessPolicyDefault
Write-Host 'Tenant Restrictions state:'
Write-Host ($trPolicy.TenantRestrictions | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 4)
KQL: Auditing Tenant Restriction Violations

Microsoft Entra ID logs tenant restriction violations in the SignInLogs table. The ResultType 900432 indicates that a sign-in was blocked by the tenant restrictions policy, allowing security teams to detect and investigate shadow IT access attempts.
Detect Blocked Sign-In Attempts

// Tenant Restrictions violations — employees attempting to authenticate to blocked tenants
SignInLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(7d)
| where ResultType == 900432
| extend BlockedTenantId  = tostring(parse_json(AuthenticationDetails)[0].authenticationStepResultDetail)
| extend TargetApp        = AppDisplayName
| extend UserPrincipal    = UserPrincipalName
| extend SourceIP         = IPAddress
| summarize
    Attempts  = count(),
    FirstSeen = min(TimeGenerated),
    LastSeen  = max(TimeGenerated)
    by UserPrincipal, ResourceTenantId, TargetApp, SourceIP
| order by Attempts desc
Top Users Bypassing Tenant Restrictions

// Identify repeat offenders — employees with the most blocked attempts
SignInLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(30d)
| where ResultType == 900432
| summarize
    BlockedAttempts  = count(),
    UniqueExternalTenants = dcount(ResourceTenantId),
    LastAttempt      = max(TimeGenerated)
    by UserPrincipalName, UserDisplayName
| order by BlockedAttempts desc
| take 25
New External Tenants Being Accessed

// Track which external tenant IDs employees are attempting to reach
// Useful for identifying SaaS apps that may need to be added to the allow list
SignInLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(14d)
| where ResultType == 900432
| summarize
    Attempts       = count(),
    UniqueUsers    = dcount(UserPrincipalName),
    SampleUsers    = make_set(UserPrincipalName, 5)
    by ResourceTenantId, AppDisplayName
| order by Attempts desc
Tenant Restrictions and Conditional Access Integration

Tenant Restrictions policies work alongside Conditional Access but serve different enforcement layers. Tenant Restrictions block authentication to unauthorized external tenants at the network/identity plane, while Conditional Access controls how users authenticate to your own tenant.
Scenario
Mechanism
Policy Layer
Enforced By
Employee authenticates to your tenant
Conditional Access
Inbound (your tenant)
Entra ID Conditional Access
Employee authenticates to another company's tenant
Tenant Restrictions
Outbound (employee network)
Proxy or Global Secure Access
External user accesses your tenant
Cross-Tenant Access Policy (inbound)
Inbound from partner
Entra ID CTAP
Your user accesses a partner tenant
Cross-Tenant Access Policy (outbound)
Outbound to partner
Entra ID CTAP
Personal Microsoft account sign-in blocked
Tenant Restrictions v1 (AllowedDirectories)
HTTP header injection
Corporate proxy
Consumer account blocked (TRv2)
Tenant Restrictions v2 (external identities block)
Token-level signal
GSA or compatible proxy
Common Issues and Troubleshooting
Symptom
Root Cause
Resolution
Legitimate SaaS app blocked unexpectedly
SaaS app's Entra tenant is not in the allow list
Add the SaaS vendor's tenant ID to the partner exceptions in CTAP or the v1 header allow list
TR headers not injected (v1)
Proxy SSL inspection not configured for login.microsoftonline.com
Verify ssl_bump rules target all MS login domains; check proxy logs for TLS passthrough
ResultType 900432 not appearing in logs
Sign-in logs not yet flowing to Log Analytics
Check diagnostic settings in Entra ID → Monitoring → Diagnostic settings; ensure SignInLogs is sent to the workspace
GSA client not routing auth traffic
Traffic forwarding profile for Microsoft 365 not enabled
In Entra Internet Access, enable the Microsoft 365 traffic forwarding profile
Personal accounts still work on managed devices
TRv1 only blocks organizational accounts by default
Add AllowedPersonalAccounts header value 'None' to also block personal Microsoft accounts
Partner tenant users cannot access shared resources
CTAP outbound policy blocks all external access
Create a per-partner exception in CTAP outbound settings allowing the specific partner tenant
Summary
Tenant Restrictions prevent employees from using corporate networks/devices to authenticate to unauthorized Azure AD tenants, blocking data exfiltration to personal accounts or shadow-IT SaaS.
v1 works by HTTP proxy injecting Restrict-Access-To-Tenants and Restrict-Access-Context headers; requires TLS inspection on the proxy.
v2 (Entra Internet Access) enforces restrictions at Microsoft's edge using a client-side agent, covering both sign-in and already-cached tokens, including remote workers off the corporate network.
Exceptions for specific partner tenants are configured in Cross-Tenant Access Settings as per-partner entries that override the default block policy.
Sign-in blocks appear in Entra sign-in logs as ResultType 900432 (AADSTS900432); KQL queries over SignInLogs expose violation trends.
